transcript.clean_text: It’s the first summit finish of La Vuelta! Welcome to highlights of stage 5, right here on GCN Racing. Yesterday, we had 2nd consecutive bunch sprint, and it couldn’t have been closer on the line. 22 year old Dutch National Champion Fabio Jakobsen pipping Irish Champion Sam Bennett by a matter of millimetres, to take his first Grand Tour stage win, in his first Grand Tour. Overall, it’s still Nicolas Roche of Team Sunweb in the lead, by that same 2 second margin over Nairo Quintana. Today was all about the climbers. 170.7km finishing at the Javalambre Observatory, 1950m above sea level, up a climb known as the peak of the vulture. It’s a tough one that has never been used before - the last 5kms averaging over 10%. Despite this one looking good for a breakaway, only three riders set off up the road this morning. Jose Herrada of Team Cofidis was the best placed on GC at 13 minutes 21 seconds. He was joined by the Burgos BH pairing of Jetse Bol and, current KOM leader and prolific breakaway entrant, Angel Madrazo. Madrazo had secured the maximum 5 points atop the first and second climbs of the day, but on the third, he was beginning to pay for his efforts of the last few days, unable to stay with the pace being set by Herrada He didn’t give up, though - he pushed on over the top of the climb and made his way back up to the head of the race. And by this point, it was beginning to look possible, almost 10 minutes the gap between break and bunch with 40kms remaining. Behind, things were reasonably calm in the bunch, with everyone seemingly content to just wait for the final climb to the finish. Bol would take the maximum points and three bonus seconds at the intermediate sprint in Manzenara, as the gap continued to rise. And it was close to 11 minutes as this nasty incident occurred - 2 riders from Bahrain Merida going down hard and causing a chain reaction behind. Phil Bauhaus was hurt the worst and was soon being treated at the race doctors car. Thankfully, there didn’t appear to be any broken bones, but that road rash is going to sting tonight. EF Education First had a number of cards to play today - Uran, Higuita and Hugh Carthy all looking to be on decent form at the moment. Approaching the final climb, Madrazo was almost taken out by his own team car, who hit his back wheel, flinging him off line and into Herrada, who himself came close to hitting the rocks on the side of the road. That was a close call, and the DS would have been breathing a big sigh of relief that it wasn’t worse. As the leaders approached the final climb, the gap to the bunch was down to under 9 minutes - still a decent buffer, but having been out there all day, it’d be touch and go as to whether they’d take the stage, particularly with the likes of Team Ineos driving things at warp speed behind. With 6.5kms to go, it looked to be game over for Madrazo, not a surprised given how many breakaways he’s been in at this year’s race. Behind, there was a brief slowing as they waited for the steeper final part of the climb. This, however, is Madrazo back on the attack! You can’t keep this guy down - whatever happened, he was doing an amazing job for his teammate Bol, who was able to sit on Herrada’s wheel. Astana were the next team to hit the front, looking to take some of the time back that Lopez lost to some key rivals on stage 2. Known as Mclovin, I think Madrazo’s new nickname could be YoYo, that’s exactly what he was doing out front, dropped yet again and watching the other two sail up the road. Hugh Carthy was set to work on the front of the GC Group to help Rigoberto Uran, whittling the group down and dropping the likes of De La Cruz, but also his own teammate Higuita. World Champion Alejandro Valverde was clearly feeling good, he was the first of the big names to put in a proper attack, and for a while, there was no reaction. Keldermann would also dropped, as would his teammate and race leader Nico Roche, the Irishman in a group here with Uran, Majka and Chaves. Sepp Kuss was on a flyer, today, it was the young American who brought Valverde back, doing an amazing job for Roglic. By this point, it was game over for Roche - his hopes of holding onto the jersey had gone. It was only a matter of time before this happened, wasn’t it? Miguel Angel Lopez, in the best young riders jersey, putting in a huuuuge attack with 3kms to the top - nobody else able to follow. This was one of the rides of the day, though - Tadej Pogocar, the youngest rider in the race, just a few seconds behind Valverde, who’d gone off in pursuit of Lopez with Roglic for company. Meanwhile, the break, by this point, looked almost certain to take the stage. But would it be betweem two, or three? Madrazo was once again clawing his way back into contention. Lopez was flying, though - he’s never won a Grand Tour, but with the form he was showing today, you have to wonder whether this is going to be his time. Into the closing kilometre and Madrazo had not only made his way back on, he’d gone to the front. And this was the moment he attacked, immediately putting a gap betweem himself and Herrada, what a monumental effort from the Spaniard! A quick glance over his shoulder and he could see that he was almost assured of the win, and in fact his accelaration was so impressive, he even had time to sit up and raise his arms aloft as the winner of stage 5. Incredible stuff! Bol would place 2nd, Herrada third, but it wasn’t long before this man would make his way across the line in 4th, Lopez sending out a strong message to his rivals on the first mountain top finish of the race. He’d put 12 seconds into Valverde and Roglic. Behind, Quintana was doing his best to limit his losses to the teammate who attacked him, coming in with Kuss and Chaves a further 42 seconds back. If you ever want a lesson in perseverance and never giving up, this is it. Angel Madrazo has been in a break on 3 of the 4 road stages at this year’s Vuelta, he was dropped on the penultimate climb, dropped multiple times on the last climb, and still won, and I, for one, am extremely happy for him! It was a valiant effort from Roche, but he’d drop to 5th place on GC at the end of the stage. 6x and the trio had built an advantage of over 7 minutes in the first hour of racing, as Team Sunweb set an easy pace behind. If BH Burgos win - the sort of win that can keep a small team’s sponsorship going for another couple of years. Tomorrow could also be described as a summit finish, although it is nowhere near as hard as today’s. A shade under 200kms, there are 4 climbs, 2 at the start, and 2 near the finish. This one really does have breakaway written all over it. I spy, with my little eye, something beginning with TDG. Thomas de Gendt for the win tomorrow! Well done to X for correctly predicting today’s winner.
